title: Finding errors in study conclusions
description: Practice identifying which principals of good experiment design have or haven't been met in a study.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jerry was reading about a study that looked at the connection between smartphone usage and happiness based on data from approximately $5{,}000$ randomly selected teenagers. The study found that, on average, the teens who spent more time on smartphones were significantly less happy than those who spent less time on smartphones.

Jerry concluded that spending more time on smartphones makes teens less happy.

**Why is Jerry's conclusion not appropriate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When a study finds a statistically significant relationship, we can look at the study design (observational vs. experimental) to determine whether or not we can make conclusions about cause and effect.

If the sample used for the study is representative of a larger population, we can extend the results to the larger population. A large random sample is a good way to get a representative sample.

|| **What conclusions are appropriate?** ||
Study design | Sample $\blueD{\text{represents}}$ population | Sample $\purpleC{\text{doesn't represent}}$ population
- | - | -
$\greenD{\text{Randomized experiment}}$ | $\greenD{\text{Causal}}$ relationship; results $\blueD{\text{apply to population}}$. | $\greenD{\text{Causal}}$ relationship; results $\purpleC{\text{only apply to sample}}$.
$\maroonD{\text{Observational study}}$ | $\maroonD{\text{No causal}}$ relationship; results $\blueD{\text{apply to population}}$. | $\maroonD{\text{No causal}}$ relationship; results $\purpleC{\text{only apply to sample}}$.

Jerry concluded that spending more time on smartphones *makes* teens less happy, but this was an $\maroonD{\text{observational study}}$, not a randomized experiment. We shouldn't make cause and effect conclusions like this from observational studies, because other variables may be causing the relationship, or the causation may be reversed.

For example, it's possible that other factors cause the teens' happiness, and being less happy causes increased smartphone usage.

To conclude that spending more time on smartphones makes teens less happy, we'd need to carry out an experiment that randomly assigned teens to varying levels of smartphone usage.
Jerry's conclusion is not appropriate because the study was observational, so he shouldn't make a causal conclusion.

choices.content: The study was observational, so the sample size isn't large enough to support his conclusion.
The study was observational, so he shouldn't make a causal conclusion.
The study was an experiment, but it didn't mention  the usage of devices other than smartphones.
The study was an experiment, but it didn't group teens into blocks based on previous smartphone usage.
The study wasn't a census, which is the best way to establish causation.

assessment_items.question_content:
Diego read a medical research study about the connection between type 2 diabetes and walnut consumption. The study looked at dietary and disease patterns in a sample of over $30{,}000$ American adults, and it found that those who consumed more walnuts were significantly less likely to have type 2 diabetes.

Diego concluded that eating more walnuts would make him less likely to get type 2 diabetes.

**Why is Diego's conclusion not appropriate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When a study finds a statistically significant relationship, we can look at the study design (observational vs. experimental) to determine whether or not we can make conclusions about cause and effect.

If the sample used for the study is representative of a larger population, we can extend the results to the larger population. A large random sample is a good way to get a representative sample.

|| **What conclusions are appropriate?** ||
Study design | Sample $\blueD{\text{represents}}$ population | Sample $\purpleC{\text{doesn't represent}}$ population
- | - | -
$\greenD{\text{Randomized experiment}}$ | $\greenD{\text{Causal}}$ relationship; results $\blueD{\text{apply to population}}$. | $\greenD{\text{Causal}}$ relationship; results $\purpleC{\text{only apply to sample}}$.
$\maroonD{\text{Observational study}}$ | $\maroonD{\text{No causal}}$ relationship; results $\blueD{\text{apply to population}}$. | $\maroonD{\text{No causal}}$ relationship; results $\purpleC{\text{only apply to sample}}$.

Diego concluded that eating more walnuts would *make* him less likely to get type 2 diabetes, but this was an $\maroonD{\text{observational study}}$, not a randomized experiment. We shouldn't make cause and effect conclusions like this from observational studies, because other variables may be causing the relationship, or the causation may be reversed.

For example, it's possible people who eat more walnuts also eat healthier food in general, and that makes them less likely to get type 2 diabetes.

To conclude that eating more walnuts makes adults less likely to get type 2 diabetes, we'd need to carry out an experiment that randomly assigned adults to varying levels of walnut consumption.
Diego's conclusion is not appropriate because the study was observational, so he shouldn't make a causal conclusion.

choices.content: The study was observational, so the sample size isn't large enough to support his conclusion.
The study was observational, so he shouldn't make a causal conclusion.
The study was an experiment, but it didn't mention  other foods and their relationship with type 2 diabetes.
The study was an experiment, but it didn't group adults into blocks based on family medical history.
The study wasn't a census, which is the best way to establish causation.

assessment_items.question_content:
Julia read about a study involving migraine headaches in men. The study of over $100$ men found that those with migraines had higher estrogen levels, on average, than men who were migraine-free. Both groups of men had similar testosterone levels.

Julia concluded that estrogen caused these men's migraines.

**Why is Julia's conclusion not appropriate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When a study finds a statistically significant relationship, we can look at the study design (observational vs. experimental) to determine whether or not we can make conclusions about cause and effect.

If the sample used for the study is representative of a larger population, we can extend the results to the larger population. A large random sample is a good way to get a representative sample.

|| **What conclusions are appropriate?** ||
Study design | Sample $\blueD{\text{represents}}$ population | Sample $\purpleC{\text{doesn't represent}}$ population
- | - | -
$\greenD{\text{Randomized experiment}}$ | $\greenD{\text{Causal}}$ relationship; results $\blueD{\text{apply to population}}$. | $\greenD{\text{Causal}}$ relationship; results $\purpleC{\text{only apply to sample}}$.
$\maroonD{\text{Observational study}}$ | $\maroonD{\text{No causal}}$ relationship; results $\blueD{\text{apply to population}}$. | $\maroonD{\text{No causal}}$ relationship; results $\purpleC{\text{only apply to sample}}$.

Julia concluded that estrogen *caused* these men's migraines, but this was an $\maroonD{\text{observational study}}$, not a randomized experiment. We shouldn't make cause and effect conclusions like this from observational studies, because other variables may be causing the relationship, or the causation may be reversed.

For example, it's possible that the men's bodies produced estrogen in response to their migraine headaches.

To conclude that estrogen caused migraines in men, we'd need to carry out an experiment that randomly assigned men to varying levels of estrogen.
Julia's conclusion is not appropriate because the study was observational, so she shouldn't make a causal conclusion.

choices.content: The study was observational, so the sample size isn't large enough to support her conclusion.
The study was observational, so she shouldn't make a causal conclusion.
The study was an experiment, but it didn't mention  the subject's diets.
The study was an experiment, but it didn't group the men into blocks based on age.
The study wasn't a census, which is the best way to establish causation.

assessment_items.question_content:
A class was curious if their teacher could grade and return double-spaced papers faster than single-spaced papers. For a certain assignment, each student flipped a coin to determine if they would single-space or double-space their paper. The students agreed to all use the same font. The students who double-spaced received their graded papers back significantly faster than students who single-spaced.

The class concluded that any teacher can grade and return papers faster if they are double-spaced rather than single-spaced.

**Why is their conclusion not appropriate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When a study finds a statistically significant relationship, we can look at the study design (observational vs. experimental) to determine whether or not we can make conclusions about cause and effect.

If the sample used for the study is representative of a larger population, we can extend the results to the larger population. A large random sample is a good way to get a representative sample.

|| **What conclusions are appropriate?** ||
Study design | Sample $\blueD{\text{represents}}$ population | Sample $\purpleC{\text{doesn't represent}}$ population
- | - | -
$\greenD{\text{Randomized experiment}}$ | $\greenD{\text{Causal}}$ relationship; results $\blueD{\text{apply to population}}$. | $\greenD{\text{Causal}}$ relationship; results $\purpleC{\text{only apply to sample}}$.
$\maroonD{\text{Observational study}}$ | $\maroonD{\text{No causal}}$ relationship; results $\blueD{\text{apply to population}}$. | $\maroonD{\text{No causal}}$ relationship; results $\purpleC{\text{only apply to sample}}$.

The class concluded that *any* teacher can grade and return papers faster if they are double-spaced rather than single-spaced, but their study only included *their* teacher. Even though their teacher graded and returned double-spaced papers faster, their teacher probably $\purpleC{\text{doesn't represent}}$ all teachers.

To conclude that *any* teacher can grade and return double-spaced papers faster, we'd need to carry out this experiment on a representative sample of teachers.
Their conclusion is not appropriate because the study was an experiment, but it didn't include anyone other than their teacher.

choices.content: The study was observational, so the sample size isn't large enough to support their conclusion.
The study was observational, so they shouldn't make a causal conclusion.
The study was an experiment, but it didn't include anyone other than their teacher.
The study was an experiment, but it didn't group the students into blocks based their grades on previous assignments.
The study wasn't a census, which is the best way to establish causation.

assessment_items.question_content:
There is an ongoing debate about how many spaces should be placed after a period in typed documents. Alana read about a study where $100$ participants all read the same document typed in Courier New font. Half of the participants were randomly assigned the document with one space after each period, and the other half were given the document with two spaces after each period.

Participants who read the document with two spaces after each period were able to finish reading significantly faster than those with one space after each period. Alana concluded that using two spaces after each period will help people read all documents faster.

**Why is Alana's conclusion not appropriate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When a study finds a statistically significant relationship, we can look at the study design (observational vs. experimental) to determine whether or not we can make conclusions about cause and effect.

If the sample used for the study is representative of a larger population, we can extend the results to the larger population. A large random sample is a good way to get a representative sample.

|| **What conclusions are appropriate?** ||
Study design | Sample $\blueD{\text{represents}}$ population | Sample $\purpleC{\text{doesn't represent}}$ population
- | - | -
$\greenD{\text{Randomized experiment}}$ | $\greenD{\text{Causal}}$ relationship; results $\blueD{\text{apply to population}}$. | $\greenD{\text{Causal}}$ relationship; results $\purpleC{\text{only apply to sample}}$.
$\maroonD{\text{Observational study}}$ | $\maroonD{\text{No causal}}$ relationship; results $\blueD{\text{apply to population}}$. | $\maroonD{\text{No causal}}$ relationship; results $\purpleC{\text{only apply to sample}}$.

Alana concluded that using two spaces after each period will help people read *any* document faster, but this study only included a document typed in Courier New font. Even though people were able to read this document faster with two spaces after each period, this document $\purpleC{\text{doesn't represent}}$ all documents.

To conclude that using two spaces will help people read *any* document faster, we'd need to carry out this experiment with a broader variety of documents.
Alana's conclusion is not appropriate because the study was an experiment, but it didn't include fonts other than Courier New.

choices.content: The study was observational, so the sample size isn't large enough to support her conclusion.
The study was observational, so she shouldn't make a causal conclusion.
The study was an experiment, but it didn't include fonts other than Courier New.
The study was an experiment, but it didn't group participants into blocks based their reading abilities.
The study wasn't a census, which is the best way to establish causation.

assessment_items.question_content:
A company tested their new golf ball by having $20$ professional golfers each hit $100$ shots with the company's new ball and $100$ shots with the golfer's current ball (in a random order). The labels were removed, so the golfers didn't know which balls were which. The golfers, on average, hit their shots significantly farther with the new ball.

The company cites this study in an advertisement claiming that this new ball will help all golfers hit farther shots.

**Why is the company's claim not appropriate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When a study finds a statistically significant relationship, we can look at the study design (observational vs. experimental) to determine whether or not we can make conclusions about cause and effect.

If the sample used for the study is representative of a larger population, we can extend the results to the larger population. A large random sample is a good way to get a representative sample.

|| **What conclusions are appropriate?** ||
Study design | Sample $\blueD{\text{represents}}$ population | Sample $\purpleC{\text{doesn't represent}}$ population
- | - | -
$\greenD{\text{Randomized experiment}}$ | $\greenD{\text{Causal}}$ relationship; results $\blueD{\text{apply to population}}$. | $\greenD{\text{Causal}}$ relationship; results $\purpleC{\text{only apply to sample}}$.
$\maroonD{\text{Observational study}}$ | $\maroonD{\text{No causal}}$ relationship; results $\blueD{\text{apply to population}}$. | $\maroonD{\text{No causal}}$ relationship; results $\purpleC{\text{only apply to sample}}$.

The company is claiming that this new ball will help *any* golfer hit farther shots, but their study only included professional golfers. Even though they hit significantly farther shots on average with the new ball, these golfers $\purpleC{\text{don't represent}}$ all golfers.

To conclude that this new ball will help *any* golfer hit farther shots, we'd need to carry out this experiment with a broader sample of golfers with varying skill levels.
The company's claim is not appropriate because the study was an experiment, but it only included professional golfers.

choices.content: The study was observational, so the sample size isn't large enough to support their claim.
The study was observational, so they shouldn't make a causal conclusion.
The study was an experiment, but it only included professional golfers.
The study was an experiment, but it didn't group the golfers into blocks based on their abilities.
The study wasn't a census, which is the best way to establish causation.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ricardo suspects that his cat will prefer a new cat food to their current cat food. For his cat's next feeding, he decides to feed his cat in a different room than usual. He puts a bowl of the new food on one side of the room and a bowl of the current food on the other side of the room (determined by a coin flip). His cat chooses the new food.

Ricardo concludes that his cat prefers the new food to their current food.

**What makes Ricardo's conclusion inappropriate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A **confounding variable** is a variable that impacted the results of a study in a way that makes it unclear whether the explanatory variable or the confounding variable was responsible for the outcomes. [[☃ explanation 2]]

A well-designed experiment can reduce the impact of confounding variables.

* **Random assignment** helps create groups that are as similar as possible (other variables get roughly balanced between the groups).
* **Control** for other variables and use a comparative design so the primary difference between the groups is the presence of the explanatory variable. Use a [[☃ definition 1]] in the control group and [[☃ definition 2]] in every group, if possible. [[☃ definition 3]] designs are even better.
* Use **replication**—have as many subjects in each group as possible to reduce to role of random chance. Ideally, repeat the experiment on new subjects to see if the same results occur.
Ricardo's experiment only included a single feeding, so lack of replication is an issue. He should carry out this process over many more feedings to reduce the role of random chance in the results.
The lack of replication to other feedings makes his conclusion inappropriate.

choices.content: The random assignment of the bowl placement
The exclusion of other cat foods
The use of only his cat and not other cats
The lack of attention to the placebo effect
The lack of replication to other feedings

assessment_items.question_content:
A gym that specializes in weight loss offers its members an optional dietary program for an extra fee. To study the effectiveness of the dietary program, a manager at the gym takes a random sample of $50$ members who participate in the dietary program and $50$ members who don't. The manager finds that those who participated in the dietary program, on average, lost significantly more weight than those who didn't participate in the program in the past $3$ months.

The manager concludes that the dietary program caused the increased weight loss for the gym's members during that time period.

**What is a confounding variable that makes their conclusion inappropriate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A **confounding variable** is a variable that impacted the results of a study in a way that makes it unclear whether the explanatory variable or the confounding variable was responsible for the outcomes. [[☃ explanation 2]]

A well-designed experiment can reduce the impact of confounding variables.

* **Random assignment** helps create groups that are as similar as possible (other variables get roughly balanced between the groups).
* **Control** for other variables and use a comparative design so the primary difference between the groups is the presence of the explanatory variable. Use a [[☃ definition 1]] in the control group and [[☃ definition 2]] in every group, if possible. [[☃ definition 3]] designs are even better.
* Use **replication**—have as many subjects in each group as possible to reduce to role of random chance. Ideally, repeat the experiment on new subjects to see if the same results occur.

The $100$ members in this study weren't randomly assigned. Their personal choice of whether or not to be in the dietary program is a confounding variable. They had to pay to join the program, so it's possible that this group is more motivated in general to lose weight.
The members' choice of whether or not they participated in the dietary program is a confounding variable that makes the manager's conclusion inappropriate.

choices.content: The random samples of $50$ members
The exclusion of nonmembers in the study
The lack of replication to other time periods
The lack of attention to the placebo effect
The members' choice of whether or not they participated in the dietary program

assessment_items.question_content:
Researchers recruited participants for a wine tasting study that included a variety of wines in terms of style and price. The participants tasted a series of wines in a random order. Participants could look at the bottle for each wine so they could take its price and label information into account as they tasted and rated each wine. The participants rated the more expensive wines significantly better tasting than the less expensive wines.

The researchers carrying out the study conclude that more expensive wines taste better.

**What is a confounding variable that makes their conclusion inappropriate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A **confounding variable** is a variable that impacted the results of a study in a way that makes it unclear whether the explanatory variable or the confounding variable was responsible for the outcomes. [[☃ explanation 1]]

A well-designed experiment can reduce the impact of confounding variables.

* **Random assignment** helps create groups that are as similar as possible (other variables get roughly balanced between the groups).
* **Control** for other variables and use a comparative design so the primary difference between the groups is the presence of the explanatory variable. Use a [[☃ definition 3]] in the control group and [[☃ definition 4]] in every group, if possible. [[☃ definition 5]] designs are even better.
* Use **replication**—have as many subjects in each group as possible to reduce to role of random chance. Ideally, repeat the experiment on new subjects to see if the same results occur.

In this study, the researchers are interested in seeing if more expensive wines taste better. Participants might assume that more expensive wines are better than less expensive wines, so letting them see the prices is a confounding variable since it may influence how they perceive each wine.
The lack of blinding makes their conclusion inappropriate.

choices.content: The use of volunteers
The exclusion of beverages other than wine
The random order of wines
The lack of blinding
The lack of a control group that doesn't drink any wine

assessment_items.question_content:
A tutoring company has offices where students physically attend a test prep course. The company recently created an online test prep course, and they wonder if it produces similar or different outcomes than their in-person course. They recruit $50$ students who are willing to try the online course. On average, those $50$ students produced significantly better outcomes than students historically do in the in-person course.

The company concludes that the online course produced better outcomes than the in-person course for these students.

**Which of these concerns makes their conclusion inappropriate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A **confounding variable** is a variable that impacted the results of a study in a way that makes it unclear whether the explanatory variable or the confounding variable was responsible for the outcomes. [[☃ explanation 1]]

A well-designed experiment can reduce the impact of confounding variables.

* **Random assignment** helps create groups that are as similar as possible (other variables get roughly balanced between the groups).
* **Control** for other variables and use a comparative design so the primary difference between the groups is the presence of the explanatory variable. Use a [[☃ definition 3]] in the control group and [[☃ definition 4]] in every group, if possible. [[☃ definition 5]] designs are even better.
* Use **replication**—have as many subjects in each group as possible to reduce to role of random chance. Ideally, repeat the experiment on new subjects to see if the same results occur.

In this study, the researchers are interested in seeing if their online course produces similar or different outcomes than their in-person course. They should use a comparative design and include at least two groups: one that takes the online course, and one that takes the in-person course.

They should also use random assignment to control for personal choice between the groups. If all $50$ students volunteer to try out the online course, their motivation and willingness become confounding variables.
Some of the $50$ students should have been assigned to a control group that used in the in-person course.

choices.content: This wasn't a random sample of students.
Some of the $50$ students should have been assigned to a control group that received no tutoring.
The students weren't blocked based on previous test scores.
No blinding was used on the students or the researchers.
Some of the $50$ students should have been assigned to a control group that used in the in-person course.

assessment_items.question_content:
Katie has $2$ types of dog treats—chicken and beef—and she wonders which type her dog prefers. She carries out an experiment where she offers her dog both treats at the same time. For consistency, she always puts the chicken treat in her right hand and the beef treat in her left hand. Her dog chooses the chicken treat in $85$ of $100$ attempts.

Katie concludes that her dog prefers the chicken treats over the beef treats.

**What is a confounding variable that makes her conclusion inappropriate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A **confounding variable** is a variable that impacted the results of a study in a way that makes it unclear whether the explanatory variable or the confounding variable was responsible for the outcomes. [[☃ explanation 2]]

A well-designed experiment can reduce the impact of confounding variables.

* **Random assignment** helps create groups that are as similar as possible (other variables get roughly balanced between the groups).
* **Control** for other variables and use a comparative design so the primary difference between the groups is the presence of the explanatory variable. Use a [[☃ definition 1]] in the control group and [[☃ definition 2]] in every group, if possible. [[☃ definition 3]] designs are even better.
* Use **replication**—have as many subjects in each group as possible to reduce to role of random chance. Ideally, repeat the experiment on new subjects to see if the same results occur.

Katie always put the same type of treat in each hand. It's possible that her dog favors her right hand and will tend to choose that side regardless of the treat. Randomizing which treat goes in which hand each attempt would control against this.
The lack of randomization in each attempt makes Katie's conclusion inappropriate.

choices.content: The lack of randomization in each attempt
The exclusion of other types of treats
The lack of replication to other dogs
The lack of double-blinding
The lack of attention to the placebo effect

assessment_items.question_content:
An education researcher wonders if young students better learn arithmetic facts using physical flashcards or a new online game. The researcher recruits $50$ students for a study. The study allows parents to choose which learning tool they'd like their child to use: flashcards or the online game. Each student takes a pre-test and a post-test, and at the end of the study, students who used flashcards show significantly better outcomes than students who used the online game.

The researcher concludes that flashcards were more effective than the online game for this group of students.

**What is a confounding variable that makes their conclusion inappropriate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A **confounding variable** is a variable that impacted the results of a study in a way that makes it unclear whether the explanatory variable or the confounding variable was responsible for the outcomes. [[☃ explanation 2]]

A well-designed experiment can reduce the impact of confounding variables.

* **Random assignment** helps create groups that are as similar as possible (other variables get roughly balanced between the groups).
* **Control** for other variables and use a comparative design so the primary difference between the groups is the presence of the explanatory variable. Use a [[☃ definition 1]] in the control group and [[☃ definition 2]] in every group, if possible. [[☃ definition 3]] designs are even better.
* Use **replication**—have as many subjects in each group as possible to reduce to role of random chance. Ideally, repeat the experiment on new subjects to see if the same results occur.

The $50$ students in this study weren't randomly assigned. Their parents' choice of which learning tool their child uses is a confounding variable. The parents who chose the flashcards might be more willing to help their child with that tool than the online game.
The parents' choice of which learning tool their child uses is a confounding variable that makes the researcher's conclusion inappropriate.

choices.content: The use of volunteers
The exclusion of other math learning tools in the study
The lack of blinding in the students
The lack of a control group that uses no learning tool
The parents' choice of which learning tool their child uses
